---
title: Introduction to Markdown
sidebar: markdown-intro-sidebar
---

Markdown is a simple way of writing formatted text using plain characters. It is useful because the same ideas show up later in Jupyter notebooks, README files, and many scientific writing tools.

The goal here is not to learn every Markdown feature. It is to learn a small, controlled subset:

1. Headings
2. Bold and italic text
3. Lists
4. Links
5. Inline code and code blocks

## Try Markdown

Edit the text on the left and watch the rendered result on the right.

```{=html}
<div class="markdown-playground">
<div class="markdown-playground__grid">
  <div class="markdown-playground__panel">
    <div class="markdown-playground__toolbar">
      <span>Markdown Input</span>
      <button type="button" class="btn btn-sm btn-outline-secondary" data-markdown-reset>Reset</button>
    </div>
    <textarea class="markdown-playground__input" data-markdown-input spellcheck="false"># My first Markdown note

This is **bold text**, this is *italic text*, and this is `inline code`.

## Shopping list

- apples
- bananas
- coffee

## Steps

1. Open the page
2. Edit the text
3. Look at the preview

> Markdown lets you write simple formatted text without using many buttons.

Visit [Quarto](https://quarto.org) to see another Markdown-based tool.
```python
print("Hello from a code block")
```</textarea>
  </div>
  <div class="markdown-playground__panel">
    <div class="markdown-playground__toolbar">
      <span>Rendered Preview</span>
    </div>
    <div class="markdown-playground__preview" data-markdown-preview></div>
  </div>
</div>
</div>
```

## Quick Reference

| You write | It becomes |
| --- | --- |
| `# Heading` | Large heading |
| `## Heading` | Smaller heading |
| `**word**` | **word** |
| `*word*` | *word* |
| `` `code` `` | `code` |
| `- item` | Bullet point |
| `1. item` | Numbered list |
| `[text](https://example.com)` | Link |

## Teaching Use

This editor is intentionally smaller and stricter than a full notebook environment. That fits your course goal well: students can practice the core idea of Markdown without being distracted by notebook controls, menus, or file state.

Later, when they move to Google Colab or Jupyter, they can transfer two ideas at once:

1. A code cell contains Python.
2. A text cell contains Markdown.
